description: The third-person singular pronoun, marking one who is neither speaker nor addressee. More than indicating absence from the conversation, 'shae' acknowledges the vast world of others who exist in their fullness whether or not we address them directly, whose stories continue beyond our speaking.
sound_symbolism: The word begins with 'sha', where the soft fricative 'ʃ' whispers acknowledgment without intrusion—the gentlest way to speak of those not present to speak for themselves. The open 'a' holds space for their undefined presence. This spacious acknowledgment then focuses through 'e', giving specific shape to reference without claiming to capture essence. The acoustic journey from soft acknowledgment through open space to gentle focus mirrors ethical reference—speaking of others with the same care we would want for ourselves when absent.
grammatical_notes: Functions as the third-person singular pronoun without gender marking—the same form refers to all beings regardless of gender identity. No case marking maintains consistency across syntactic positions. Cannot take modifiers. The lack of animacy distinction means 'shae' can refer to people, animals, or even sacred objects, acknowledging that personhood exceeds human categories. Explicitly non-binary, offering linguistic refuge from gender's false dichotomy.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: By refusing gender marking, 'shae' removes a major source of linguistic violence—the forced categorization of others into boxes that may not fit. The soft fricative makes reference gentle, acknowledging that speaking of the absent requires special care.
pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: The word embodies right speech about others—acknowledging existence without claiming knowledge of essence. Like the teaching to avoid gossip and harsh speech, 'shae' makes it structurally easier to reference others without judgment or assumption.
pillars.solarpunk-values: Building inclusive futures requires language that makes space for all identities. 'Shae' serves this vision by providing a pronoun that works for everyone, supporting the flourishing of diverse genders and ways of being without linguistic constraint.
